---
title: "Bayesian Adaptation"
---

## Adaptation Curve

Performance as a function of the number of local grab samples used for Bayesian adaptation. The curve shows how quickly per-site accuracy improves compared to traditional OLS regression.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-adaptation-curve
from pathlib import Path
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, ".")
from _scripts.figures import make_adaptation_curve

fig = make_adaptation_curve(Path("dashboard_data/summary.json"))
fig.show()
```

## How Shrinkage Works

The Bayesian method balances global model predictions against local calibration data. With few samples, it trusts the global model; with more samples, it shifts toward the local correction. The Student-t prior is more conservative than Gaussian at small N (protects against outlier calibration samples) but converges faster at large N for sites with unusual SSC/turbidity ratios.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-weight-curve
from _scripts.figures import make_bayesian_weight_curve

fig = make_bayesian_weight_curve()
fig.show()
```

## Zero-Shot Baseline by Site Quality

Three holdout sites showing how the model performs at different quality levels before any local calibration. Each panel shows observed vs predicted SSC at zero-shot.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-shrinkage-viz
from _scripts.figures import make_bayesian_shrinkage_viz

fig = make_bayesian_shrinkage_viz(
    Path("dashboard_data/per_reading.parquet"),
    Path("dashboard_data/summary.json"),
)
fig.show()
```

## Adaptation Surprise

Which sites benefit most from adaptation? Sites in the upper-left quadrant were poorly predicted at zero-shot but improved dramatically with calibration samples.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-adaptation-surprise
from _scripts.figures import make_adaptation_surprise

fig = make_adaptation_surprise(Path("dashboard_data/per_site.parquet"))
fig.show()
```
